name: pendulum
what_it_does: DateTime library for Python with timezone-aware dates and intuitive API — drop-in replacement for Python datetime with richer functionality. pendulum features: pendulum.now(timezone) for current time, pendulum.parse() for string parsing, pendulum.datetime() with timezone, add()/subtract() for arithmetic, diff() for Duration objects, diff_for_humans() for human-readable ('3 hours ago'), in_timezone() for conversion, start_of('day')/end_of('week'), period() for date ranges, isoformat() and format() with custom patterns, and immutable DateTime objects. Handles DST transitions and ambiguous times correctly.
use_cases: Agent timezone-aware scheduling — import pendulum; now = pendulum.now('America/New_York'); next_run = now.add(hours=4).start_of('hour'); print(f'Next run: {next_run.diff_for_humans()}') — timezone-aware datetime; agent scheduler works across timezone boundaries; diff_for_humans() produces 'in 4 hours' for display; handles DST transitions automatically

Agent datetime arithmetic — deadline = pendulum.parse('2024-03-15T09:00:00-05:00'); remaining = deadline.diff(pendulum.now('UTC')); print(f'{remaining.in_hours()} hours remaining') — parse ISO8601 with timezone; diff() returns Duration; agent countdown timer correctly handles timezone offsets; Duration.in_hours() for fractional hours

Agent log timestamp parsing — entry_time = pendulum.parse(log_line['timestamp']); age = pendulum.now('UTC').diff(entry_time); if age.in_minutes() > 60: flag_stale_entry(entry) — parse log timestamps with timezone; agent log analysis compares entries across timezone-aware timestamps; avoids naive datetime comparison bugs

Agent date range iteration — period = pendulum.period(pendulum.datetime(2024, 1, 1), pendulum.datetime(2024, 12, 31)); for dt in period.range('weeks'): report = generate_weekly_report(dt) — iterate over date range; agent report generation produces weekly summaries; range() yields pendulum instances for each week

Agent human-readable display — created_at = pendulum.instance(db_record.created_at); print(created_at.diff_for_humans()) — '2 hours ago' or '3 days from now'; agent notification system shows relative times; diff_for_humans() localizes based on absolute vs relative time; automatic 'ago' vs 'from now' logic
not_for: Minimal stdlib extension — pendulum is a significant library; for simple timezone handling use datetime with zoneinfo (Python 3.9+)
Performance-critical date operations — pendulum adds overhead over stdlib datetime; for bulk date processing use pandas datetime or numpy datetime64
Non-Python environments — pendulum is Python-only; for multi-language date handling use ISO8601 strings
best_when: Python applications needing correct timezone handling, date arithmetic, and human-readable time display — pendulum's immutable timezone-aware datetime objects prevent the common DST and timezone bugs of stdlib datetime.
avoid_when: You only need simple stdlib datetime, need high-performance bulk date operations (use pandas), or want minimal dependencies (use datetime + zoneinfo).

pricing.notes: pendulum is MIT licensed. Free for all use.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits. pendulum 3.x uses Rust extensions for performance. now() and parse() are fast. diff() for Duration computation is fast. diff_for_humans() is string formatting — fast. Suitable for real-time use.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: InvalidTimezone for unknown timezone strings. ParserError for unparseable datetime strings. ValueError for invalid datetime components. pendulum.parse() is strict — malformed strings raise ParserError; use strict=False for lenient parsing. Error messages are descriptive.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: pendulum DateTime objects are immutable — operations return new instances. add()/subtract() produce new DateTime. parse() is deterministic for same string. now() is not idempotent (current time). format() is deterministic for same DateTime.
known_agent_gotchas: pendulum 3.x breaking changes from 2.x — pendulum 3.0 added Rust extension backend with breaking API changes; some 2.x methods removed or renamed; agent code upgrading from 2.x must check changelog; CHANGELOG.md lists deprecated methods; pendulum.now().add(days=1) still works but some formatting methods changed

pendulum.instance() required for stdlib datetime conversion — stdlib datetime object passed to pendulum functions raises TypeError; must convert: dt = pendulum.instance(stdlib_datetime_obj); or: pendulum.datetime(2024, 1, 1, tz='UTC'); pendulum.now() returns pendulum.DateTime not stdlib datetime; mixing both types in comparisons works but may cause subtle bugs

parse() requires timezone for correct comparison — pendulum.parse('2024-01-01T12:00:00') without timezone returns naive-aware pendulum DateTime defaulting to UTC; comparing with pendulum.now('America/New_York') may give wrong diff; always include timezone in parsed strings: '2024-01-01T12:00:00-05:00' or pass tz parameter to parse()

diff() argument order affects sign — dt1.diff(dt2) returns absolute duration (always positive); to get signed difference: use dt1.diff(dt2, absolute=False); agent countdown timer must use absolute=False to get negative duration for past deadlines; diff() default is absolute=True — positive even for past dates

start_of() and end_of() are timezone-aware — pendulum.now('America/New_York').start_of('day') returns midnight in New York timezone; agent code calculating 'start of business day' gets correct local midnight; stdlib datetime.replace(hour=0) is naive and may give wrong UTC time after timezone conversion

period() includes both endpoints — period = pendulum.period(start, end); len(list(period.range('days'))) includes both start and end dates; agent date range of 'days in January' from Jan 1 to Jan 31 produces 31 days; not 30 days as Python range() convention would suggest; adjust for exclusive end if needed: period(start, end.subtract(days=1))

security_score_components.security_notes: DateTime library with no network calls. Timezone-aware datetime handling prevents timing attacks based on DST confusion. Immutable objects prevent datetime mutation bugs. No security concerns beyond standard Python dependency management.
